narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the admissions model predicted a 66% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your law school application. Your profile shows strong points like an undergraduate GPA of 3.50—above the typical 3.21 average—and coming from an upper-middle income family, along with being a full-time student, female, and White, which align closely with many successful applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 (well below the average of 5.71) and especially your Year 3 decile of 1 (far under the 5.53 average) stood out as the biggest concerns. These low class rankings were the most influential factors pushing the model's prediction toward expecting bar failure, more so than any other aspect of your background. Next in influence came your LSAT score of 30, which is noticeably below the 36.70 average and further nudged the prediction in the unfavorable direction. Your family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender followed in descending order of impact, each contributing slightly toward the failure prediction despite some positives like your solid GPA. Compared to typical students, your early and later school deciles are in the bottom percentiles, your LSAT lags behind most, but your GPA exceeds the norm—yet the model weighed the weaker academic signals most heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential, but a data-driven flag that your high school performance patterns suggest challenges ahead on the bar. To improve your chances next time, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, as that's a changeable factor with dedicated prep. Building stronger study habits now could mimic the trajectories of students with higher deciles who pass the bar. Consider bar prep courses early in law school to counter those past grade signals. With targeted effort on test prep and academics, you could shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing—you have solid foundations to build on.
